data.title: Mattel and WWE renew global licensing agreement
data.description:
• Mattel (NASDAQ:MAT) will continue to be the worldwide licensing partner to World Wrestling Entertainment (NYSE:TKO), announcing on Monday a multi-year extension to their current agreement, keeping Mattel (NASDAQ:MAT) as the exclusive manufacturer and merchandiser of WWE action figures, accessories, playsets, role play items, vehicles and vehicle accessories.
  • “WWE’s relationship with Mattel has been a cornerstone of our consumer products strategy for 15 years,” said Alex Varga, co-head of revenue at WWE. 
  • Mattel’s (NASDAQ:MAT) WWE action figures continue to rank among the top 10 properties in the U.S. action figure category, and expected to expand further with WWE’s international expansion through its partnership with Netflix (NFLX).
  • Financial terms of the extension were not disclosed.

data.title: Millrose Properties nabs new Buy rating at Citi after spinoff from Lennar
data.description:
Citi started coverage of Millrose Properties (NYSE:MRP), a land banking REIT that was spun off from homebuilder Lennar (LEN) in February, with a Buy rating as analyst Eric Wolfe points to a stock offering +11% covered dividend yield with the potential for "considerable multiple expansion."

While the company has an external management structure, which Citi generally dislikes, Wolfe highlights some mitigating factors, such as Lennar (LEN) is contributing a valuable business to public markets that was only available to private equity investors; the management agreement is straightforward; and the structure is reflected in its current valuation.

Risks include: external management structures often favor asset growth over total shareholder return; homebuilders can terminate Millrose Properties' (NYSE:MRP) option agreements, which can result in losses if the company isn't able to recoup the full value; and Lennar (LEN) will be MRP's largest initial customer and shareholder with "founder rights" that shift some risks to MRP. 

Catalysts that are expected to offset the risks include: Millrose Properties (MRP) should be able to generate low double-digit unlevered returns on new investment, accretive considering MRP's debt cost; its ability to recover a high percentage of value from terminated option contracts: and the expectation it can obtain customers using its external manager's relationships, which will reduce its reliance on Lennar (LEN).

MRP is trading at ~11.2% dividend yield ~8.6x next 12-month earnings, and an ~35% discount to equity book value. Citi's $30 price target on Millrose (MRP) is based on an ~9.2% yield on Citi's estimate of MRP's dividend a year from now, ~10.8x 2026 EPS estimate, and an ~16% discount to estimate equity book value.

Millrose Properties (MRP) stock rose 1.1% in midday trading on Monday.

data.title: Mirum gets FDA nod for tablet version of liver drug, Livmarli
data.description:
• Mirum Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:MIRM) announced Monday that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved a new tablet formulation of its liver disease therapy Livmarli (maralixibat).
  • Accordingly, the orally administered therapy will be available in tablet form to treat cholestatic pruritus linked to pediatric liver diseases, Alagille syndrome, and progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis (PFIC).
  • In 2021, the FDA approved the ileal bile acid transporter inhibitor as a liquid formulation for cholestatic pruritus in patients with Alagille syndrome and expanded its use in 2024 for those with PFIC.
  • Following the latest approval, the company intends to launch Livmarli tablets in June through its direct-to-consumer platform, Mirum Access Plus.
  • “We have had tremendous success with LIVMARLI since its launch, and we hope that the availability of the tablet will provide convenience that positively impacts patients’ lives,” said Peter Radovich, MIRM’s chief operating officer.

data.title: This is why Oppenheimer is still bullish on U.S. equities amid recent volatility
data.description:
Oppenheimer analysts maintain a bullish stance on equities, as the S&P 500’s (SP500) resilience since its Oct. 27, 2023 low, “has repeatedly deflected volatility” amid broad market rotations.

In a note, John Stoltzfus, chief investment strategist at Oppenheimer Asst Management, attributes this strength to investors’ reluctance to exit positions entirely due to FOMO, framing intermittent pullbacks as mere “trims” rather than sustained declines.

The note highlights Stoltzfus’ view that underlying fundamentals — including steady consumer spending, corporate earnings growth, and robust job markets — have fortified the bull market.

Despite aggressive Fed rate hikes from 0-0.25% to 5.25-5.50% since March 2022, Stoltzfus said “the economy did not dip into recession,” underscoring policymakers’ success in balancing inflation control with economic stability.

Stoltzfus likens today’s technological advancements, particularly AI, to the transformative impact of Henry Ford’s (F) assembly line, stating they drive “greater efficiencies of execution” across industries.

He also said his favored sectors include technology (XLK), communications (XLC), consumer discretionary (XLY), financials (XLF), and industrials (XLI), while stressing diversification, noting “technology is likely to share center stage” as a cross-sector efficiency driver.

Looking ahead, Stoltzfus said small-caps (NYSEARCA:IWM), (NYSEARCA:SPSM) and mid-cap stocks (NYSEARCA:SPMD) could rally if the Fed enacts modest rate cuts later this year.

"We suggest investors look for 'babies that get thrown out with the bathwater' when downdrafts occur in the markets," he said, as there are always undervalued opportunities during downturns.

data.title: Qorvo appoints board members amid pressure from activist Starboard
data.description:
Qorvo (NASDAQ:QRVO) said it appointed two new independent board members as the chipmaker faces pressure from activist investor Starboard Value. Qorvo rose 3.2%

Qorvo (NASDAQ:QRVO) appointed Richard L. Clemmer and Christopher R. Koopmans to the board, effective immediately, according to a statement on Monday. 

The new board members come after Starboard disclosed a 7.7% stake in Qorvo in January.  Shares of Qorvo (QRVO), a supplier of radio frequency chips to Apple and Samsung, dropped 37% last year. 

"These director appointments reflect our ongoing commitment to refreshing the Board with new independent directors who bring skillsets needed to oversee Qorvo’s strategic direction and follow engagement with our shareholders, including Starboard," Walden C. Rhines, Chairman of Qorvo’s  board said in the statement. 

Clemmer is the founding partner of Socratic Partners, a semiconductor-only venture fund. He also worked at NXP Semiconductor, where he was CEO and President. Koopmans is currently chief operating officer of Marvell Technology.

data.title: Exxon exploration well offshore Cyprus fails to find commercial gas
data.description:
Exxon Mobil's (NYSE:XOM) exploration well off the coast of Cyprus has been deemed non-commercial in a blow to the country's effort to develop offshore gas resources, the Cyprus Energy Ministry said Monday.

Exxon (NYSE:XOM) began drilling the Elektra-1 well in Block 5 in January, but despite initially encouraging results, subsequent evaluation work "indicate the presence of natural gas in non-commercial quantities," the ministry said.

A drillship deployed by the Exxon-led (XOM) consortium will move to another block in Cyprus' exclusive economic zone to conduct exploration drilling at another well, named Pegasus-1.

The Cyprus government has welcomed offshore exploration drilling by Exxon (XOM) and Chevron in recent years as part of a drive to become a new natural gas source for the European Union after the outbreak Ukraine war hurt supply.

data.title: More rough waters ahead for recreation and leisure space - analyst
data.description:
The recreation and leisure space is expected to remain volatile in the near-term given ongoing uncertainty surrounding tariffs and the macroeconomic environment, says Truist analyst Michael Swartz, adding that while the recent selloff appears to have largely discounted these risks, there could be further downside to the sector in the event of a deeper consumer downturn.

For those that are unable to mitigate higher costs from tariffs, Swartz estimates cost of goods sold for those would increase by 6% to 7% with annual EBITDA contraction of 35% to 40%. And in a situation where pricing was the only lever to tariffs, he estimates that prices could need to rise 4% to 5%. Under this scenario, Swartz estimates that Polaris (PII), Holley Inc (HLLY), and LCI Industries (LCII) would have the largest direct earnings risk (as a percentage of EBITDA), while Six Flag Entertainment (FUN), United Parks & Resorts (PRKS), and Lucky Strike Entertainment (LUCK) would have the least.

According to Swartz, macro resilience varies widely across the R&L space with higher-ticket, discretionary goods to be the most impacted, while a wallet shift favoring less expensive, local recreation options could be net beneficiaries in a softer spending environment.

While Truist maintains a Buy/Hold rating on names in the R&L category, price targets were lowered across the board to reflect the market sell-off. Of those, Mastercraft Boat Holdings (MCFT), OneWater Marine (ONEW), Malibu Boats (MBUU), and MarineMax (NYSE:HZO) were given some of the deepest cuts, ranging from 26% to 42%, reflecting both the share performance over the past month and vulnerability to softer consumer spending.

Shares are mostly lower within Truist’s R&L coverage with heavier losses posted for Mastercraft (MCFT), Malibu Boats (MBUU), and MarineMax (NYSE:HZO) with the latter down nearly 10%.

With MarineMax (NYSE:HZO) slated to report FQ2 results next week, the market is positioned defensively after fiscal Q1 results were negatively impacted by back-to-back hurricanes and continued economic uncertainty. This left the company with an 11% drop in revenue and higher inventory. With demand unlikely to have improved over the last three months, investors are bracing for another tough quarter for the boat and yacht retailer.

MarineMax (HZO) is expected to report a profit of $0.18 per share, unchanged from a year ago, with sales projected to be down 0.3% year-over-year to $580.7M.

data.title: Meet Slate Auto: The Jeff Bezos-backed startup looking to disrupt the electric truck market
data.description:
Slate Auto is a Michigan-based electric vehicle (EV) startup backed by Jeff Bezos, aiming to produce an affordable two-seat electric pickup truck priced around $25,000. Founded in 2022 as part of Re:Build Manufacturing, the company has been growning rapidly. 

Notable investors include Bezos, Mark Walter, the controlling owner of the LA Dodgers and CEO of Guggenheim Partners, and Thomas Tull, who is a lead investor of Re:Build Manufacturing. The Series A funding round brought in $111 million.

The company's strategy is focused on creating an affordable truck designed for first-time buyers. Inspired by iconic vehicles like the Ford Model T and Volkswagen Beetle, Slate plans to enhance profitability through customization and accessories under the slogan "We built it. You make it".

Slate Auto is headquartered in Troy, Michigan, with a design studio in Long Beach, California, and plans for a factory in Indiana. The startup has been operating in stealth mode, although it recently showcased a prototype of its pickup truck to investors. The vehicle emphasizes simplicity and practicality, aiming to appeal to a broad market while leveraging open-source software for customization

Indiana lobbying records indicate that Slate Auto aims to start vehicle production as soon as late 2026 at a manufacturing facility somewhere near Indianapolis, Indiana.

Slate Auto could be a future competitor in the electric truck market with Ford Motor (F), Rivian Automotive (RIVN), Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), and Chevrolet (GM).

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow advance as Trump spares tech sector from retaliatory tariffs
data.description:
Traders and investors on Wall Street push the major market averages higher on Monday as President Donald Trump announced temporary tariff exemptions on smartphones, computers, and other electronics primarily imported from China.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was higher by 0.3%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has climbed up 0.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was in the green by 0.3%. 

On a sector-by-sector basis, all 11 S&P sectors have moved up into positive territory with Info Tech and Communication Services leading the way. At the other end, Health Care has been the weakest performer on the day. 

Shifting to the Treasury market and yields are lower. The longer end U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 8 basis points to 4.41%. At the same time, the shorter end U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) fell 8 basis points to 3.89%. 

Wall Street’s positive momentum push has been fueled by Trump's announcement that tariffs were exempt for smartphones, computers, and other electronics mostly imported from China, providing major relief for American tech players. 

"Tariffs continued to dominate headlines over the weekend, starting with news late on Friday that smartphones, computers, and some other electronics would be exempt from U.S. reciprocal tariffs," Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said. 

"Over the weekend, Trump partially retreated from the retreat, emphasizing that a 20% tax would apply and additional unspecified taxes on technology are coming. This erratic policy making leads to investors questioning the existence of a competent plan," UBS' Paul Donovan said. 

The upcoming week is set to feature a wave of major earnings reports from top companies, including Bank of America (BAC), Netflix (NFLX), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and American Express (AXP).

On the economic front, U.S. consumers have slightly tempered their inflation expectations for the long-term even as they hike their projections on short-term inflation, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's March Survey of Consumer Expectations released on Monday.

Additionally, Atlanta Fed President Raphael Bostic and Richmond Fed President Thomas Barkin make remarks.

As for stocks that are on the move, Palantir pops 4.5% after landing a deal to supply NATO with AI-powered battle system.

data.title: We are 'nowhere near the end' of the current economic storm
data.description:
The U.S. economy is "nowhere near the end of the storm" amid shifting U.S. policies, said Win Thin, Brown Brothers Harriman’s head of currency strategy.

During a CNBC interview, Thin argued that the “U.S. exceptionalism story has really been upended” as growth-friendly deregulation and tax cuts gave way to tariffs, deportations, and DOGE-related layoffs under the current administration.

He said these policy changes have contributed significantly to market uncertainty.

Thin said he said observation “strange price action” in markets, with the dollar weakening and Treasury yields rising despite typical risk-off conditions.

While he noted solid demand at recent U.S. Treasury auctions, Thin shared his concerns about a “loss in confidence in U.S. assets” due to unpredictable policymaking. “Markets like certainty, they hate uncertainty,” he said.

In addition, policy opacity has left the Federal Reserve in a “wait and see mode,” according to Thin.

He described the dollar’s decline as “cyclical” and “man-made,” tied to three months of destabilizing measures. He said that regaining investor trust will require time and clearer economic direction from Washington.

Thin concluded that the U.S. faces a prolonged period of turbulence, telling CNBC that “we’re in the early days” of navigating these challenges.

Market participants now confront a landscape where traditional indicators like dollar strength and yield movements defy historical patterns, reflecting a “strange milieu” of disrupted expectations, he said.
